column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=Foods
#Text=
#Text=The purposes of food are to promote growth, to supply force and heat,
#Text=and to furnish material to repair the waste which is constantly taking
#Text=place in the body.
#Text=Every breath, every thought, every motion, wears out
#Text=some portion of the delicate and wonderful house in which we live.
#Text=Various vital processes remove these worn and useless particles; and to
#Text=keep the body in health, their loss must be made good by constantly
#Text=renewed supplies of material properly adapted to replenish the worn and
#Text=impaired tissues.
#Text=This renovating material must be supplied through the
#Text=medium of food and drink, and the best food is that by which the desired
#Text=end may be most readily and perfectly attained.
#Text=The great diversity in
#Text=character of the several tissues of the body, makes it necessary that
#Text=food should contain a variety of elements, in order that each part may
#Text=be properly nourished and replenished.
#Text=PREPARATION AND COOKING.
#Text=--Few articles of food show greater
#Text=difference between good and poor cooking than the various grains.
#Text=Dry,
#Text=harsh, or underdone, they are as unwholesome as unpalatable.
#Text=Like most
#Text=of the grains, wheat, with the exception of new wheat boiled whole,
#Text=should be put into boiling water and allowed to cook continuously but
#Text=slowly until done.
#Text=Any of the unground preparations require prolonged
#Text=cooking.
#Text=The average length of time and the approximate amount of water
#Text=needed in cooking _one cupful_ of the various wheat preparations in a
#Text=double boiler is stated on page 82.
#Text=_RECIPES._
#Text=
#Text=PEARL WHEAT.
#Text=--Heat a quart of water to boiling in the inner dish of
#Text=a double boiler, and stir into it one cup or one-half pint of pearl
#Text=wheat.
#Text=Let it boil rapidly until thickened and the wheat has ceased
#Text=settling, then place in the outer boiler, in which the water should be
#Text=boiling, and cook continuously from three to four hours.
#Text=CRACKED WHEAT.
#Text=--Cracked wheat may be cooked in the same manner as
#Text=pearl wheat, by using four and one-half parts of water to one of grain.
#Text=The length of time required to cook it thoroughly is about the same as
#Text=for pearl wheat.
#Text=ROLLED WHEAT.
#Text=--This preparation of wheat requires only three parts
#Text=water to one of wheat.
#Text=It should be cooked in the same way as pearled
#Text=wheat, but requires only three hours' cooking.
#Text=BOILED WHEAT (sometimes called frumenty).
#Text=--Select newly-cut wheat,
#Text=well rubbed or threshed out.
#Text=Look it over carefully, wash, and put to
#Text=cook in five times its measure of cold water.
#Text=Let it come to a boil, and
#Text=cook gently until the grains burst open, and it can be readily mashed
#Text=between the thumb and finger.
#Text=This will require from four to ten hours,
#Text=depending upon the age and variety of the wheat used.
#Text=When done, it
#Text=should be even full of a rich, thick liquor.
#Text=If necessary, add more
#Text=boiling water, but stir as little as possible.
#Text=It may be served with
#Text=cream, the same as other wheat preparations.
#Text=It is also excellent served
#Text=with lemon and other fruit sauces.
#Text=WHEAT WITH RAISINS.
#Text=--Raisins or Zante currants may be added to any
#Text=of the foregoing recipes, if desired.
#Text=The raisins or currants should be
#Text=well steamed previously, however, and stirred in lightly and evenly just
#Text=before dishing.
#Text=If cooked with the grain, they become soft, broken, and
#Text=insipid.
#Text=Figs, well steamed and chopped, may be added in the same way.
#Text=WHEAT WITH FRESH FRUIT.
#Text=--Fresh whortleberries, blueberries, and
#Text=blackberries stirred into any of the well-cooked wheat preparations just
#Text=before serving, make a very desirable addition.
#Text=A most delicious dish
#Text=may be prepared by stirring into well-cooked cracked wheat a few
#Text=spoonfuls of rather thick cream and some fresh wild blackberries.
#Text=Serve
#Text=hot.
#Text=RICE.
#Text=DESCRIPTION.
#Text=--Rice is one of the most abundantly used and most
#Text=digestible of all the cereals.
#Text=It grows wild in India, and it is
#Text=probable that this is its native home.
#Text=It is, however, now cultivated in
#Text=most tropical and sub-tropical climates, and is said to supply the
#Text=principal food for nearly one third of the human race.
#Text=It is mentioned
#Text=in history several hundred years before Christ.
#Text=According to Soyer, an
#Text=old writer on foods, the Greeks and Romans held rice in high esteem,
#Text=believing it to be a panacea for chest and lung diseases.
#Text=The grain is so largely grown and used by the Chinese that "fan," their
#Text=word for rice, has come to enter into many compound words.
#Text=A beggar is
#Text=called a "tou-fan-tee," that is, "the rice-seeking one."
#Text=The ordinary
#Text=salutation, "Che-fan," which answers to our "How do you do?"
#Text=means,
#Text="Have you eaten your rice?"
#Text=Rice requires a wet soil, and the fields in which the grain is raised,
#Text=sometimes called "paddy" fields, are periodically irrigated.
#Text=Before
#Text=ripening, the water is drained off, and the crop is then cut with a
#Text=sickle, made into shocks, stacked, threshed, and cleaned, much like
#Text=wheat.
#Text=The rice kernel is inclosed within two coverings, a course outer
#Text=husk, which is easily removed, and an inner, reddish, siliceous coating.
#Text="Paddy" is the name given in India to the rice grain when inclosed in
#Text=its husk.
#Text=The same is termed "rough rice" in this country.
#Text=The outer
#Text=husk of the rice is usually removed in the process of threshing, but the
#Text=inner red skin, or hull, adheres very closely, and is removed by rubbing
#Text=and pounding.
#Text=The rough rice is first ground between large stones, and
#Text=then conveyed into mortars, and pounded with iron-shod pestles.
#Text=Thence,
#Text=by fanning and screening, the husk is fully removed, and the grain
#Text=divided into three different grades, whole, middlings, and small whole
#Text=grains, and polished ready for market.
#Text=The middlings consist of the
#Text=larger broken pieces of the grain; the small rice, of the small
#Text=fragments mixed with the chit of the grain.
#Text=The broken rice, well dried,
#Text=is sometimes ground into flour of different degrees of fineness.
#Text=The
#Text=small rice is much sweeter and somewhat superior in point of nutritive
#Text=value to the large or head rice usually met with in commerce.
#Text=Rice is characterized by a large percentage of starch, and is so
#Text=deficient in other food elements that if used alone, unless consumed in
#Text=very large quantities, it will not furnish the requisite amount of
#Text=nitrogenous material necessary for a perfect health food.
#Text=For this
#Text=reason, it is necessary to supplement its use with some other food
#Text=containing an excess of nitrogenous elements, as peas, beans, milk, etc.
#Text=Associated with other articles rich in albuminous elements, rice is
#Text=exceedingly valuable, and one of the most easily digested foods.
#Text=Boiled
#Text=or steamed rice requires but a little over one hour for digestion.
#Text=PREPARATION AND COOKING.
#Text=--Rice needs to be thoroughly washed to
#Text=remove the earthy taste it is so apt to have.
#Text=A good way to do this is
#Text=to put it into a colander, in a deep pan of water.
#Text=Rub the rice well
#Text=with the hands, lifting the colander in and out the water, and changing
#Text=the water until it is clear; then drain.
#Text=In this way the grit is
#Text=deposited in the water, and the rice left thoroughly clean.
#Text=The best method of cooking rice is by steaming it.
#Text=If boiled in much
#Text=water, it loses a portion of its already small percentage of nitrogenous
#Text=elements.
#Text=It requires much less time for cooking than any of the other
#Text=grains.
#Text=Like all the dried grains and seeds, rice swells in cooking to
#Text=several times its original bulk.
#Text=When cooked, each grain of rice should
#Text=be separate and distinct, yet perfectly tender.
#Text=RECIPES.
#Text=STEAMED RICE.
#Text=--Soak a cup of rice in one and a fourth cups of water
#Text=for an hour, then add a cup of milk, turn into an earthen dish suitable
#Text=for serving it from at table, and place in a steam-cooker or a covered
#Text=steamer over a kettle of boiling water, and steam for an hour.
#Text=It should
#Text=be stirred with a fork occasionally, for the first ten or fifteen
#Text=minutes.
#Text=BOILED RICE (Japanese method).
#Text=--Thoroughly cleanse the rice by
#Text=washing in several waters, and soak it overnight.
#Text=In the morning, drain
#Text=it, and put to cook in an equal quantity of boiling water, that is, a
#Text=pint of water for a pint of rice.
#Text=For cooking, a stewpan with tightly
#Text=fitting cover should be used.
#Text=Heat the water to boiling, then add the
#Text=rice, and after stirring, put on the cover, which is not again to be
#Text=removed during the boiling.
#Text=At first, as the water boils, steam will
#Text=puff out freely from under the cover, but when the water has nearly
#Text=evaporated, which will be in eight to ten minutes, according to the age
#Text=and quality of the rice, only a faint suggestion of steam will be
#Text=observed, and the stewpan must then be removed from over the fire to
#Text=some place on the range, where it will not burn, to swell and dry for
#Text=fifteen or twenty minutes.
#Text=Rice to be boiled in the ordinary manner requires two quarts of boiling
#Text=water to one cupful of rice.
#Text=It should be boiled rapidly until tender,
#Text=then drained at once, and set in a moderate oven to become dry.
#Text=Picking
#Text=and lifting lightly occasionally with a fork will make it more flaky and
#Text=dry.
#Text=Care must be taken, however, not to mash the rice grains.
#Text=BREAD MADE LIGHT BY FERMENTATION.
#Text=For general use, the most convenient form of bread is usually considered
#Text=to be that made from wheat flour, raised or made light by some method of
#Text=fermentation, although in point of nutritive value and healthfulness, it
#Text=does not equal light, unfermented, or aërated bread made without the aid
#Text=of chemicals.
#Text=THE PROCESS OF FERMENTATION.
#Text=--Fermentation is a process of
#Text=decomposition, and hence more or less destructive to the substances
#Text=subjected to its influence.
#Text=When animal and vegetable substances
#Text=containing large amounts of nitrogenous elements are in a moist state
#Text=and exposed to air, they very soon undergo a change, the result of which
#Text=is decomposition or decay.
#Text=This is occasioned by the action of germs,
#Text=which feed upon nitrogenous substances, as do the various species of
#Text=fungi.
#Text=Meat, eggs, milk, and other foods rich in nitrogenous elements
#Text=can be preserved but a short time if exposed to the atmosphere.
#Text=The
#Text=carbonaceous elements are different in this respect.
#Text=When pure starch,
#Text=sugar, or fat is exposed to the air in a moistened state, they exhibit
#Text=the very little tendency to change or decay.
#Text=Yet if placed in contact
#Text=with decomposing substances containing nitrogen, they soon begin to
#Text=change, and are themselves decomposed and destroyed.
#Text=This communication
#Text=of the condition of change from one class of substances to another, is
#Text=termed fermentation.
#Text=If a fermenting substance be added to a watery
#Text=solution containing sugar, the sugar will be changed or decomposed, and
#Text=two new substances, alcohol and carbonic acid gas, are produced.
#Text=The different stages of fermentation are noted scientifically as
#Text=alcoholic, acetous, and putrefactive.
#Text=The first is the name given to the
#Text=change which takes place in the saccharine matter of the dough, which
#Text=results in the formation of alcohol and carbonic acid gas.
#Text=This same
#Text=change takes place in the saccharine matter of fruits under the proper
#Text=with conditions of warmth, air, and moisture, and is utilized in the
#Text=production of wines and fermented liquors.
#Text=In bread-making, the alcohol and carbonic acid gas produced during the
#Text=fermentation, are formed from sugar,--that originally contained in the
#Text=flour and the additional quantity formed from starch during the
#Text=fermenting process.
#Text=It is evident, therefore, that bread cannot be
#Text=fermented without some loss in natural sweetness and nutritive value,
#Text=and bread made after this method should be managed so as to deteriorate
#Text=the material as little as possible.
#Text=If this fermentation continues long enough, the acetous fermentation is
#Text=set up, and _acetic_ acid, the essential element of vinegar, is formed
#Text=and the dough becomes sour.
#Text=If the process of fermentation is very much
#Text=prolonged, the putrefactive change is set up, and the gluten is more or
#Text=less decomposed.
#Text=If the dough be baked during the alcoholic and carbonic-acid stage of
#Text=fermentation, the gas will render the loaf light and porous.
#Text=The alcohol
#Text=will be dissipated by the heat during the baking, or evaporated shortly
#Text=afterward, provided the baking be thorough.
#Text=If the fermentation is
#Text=allowed to proceed until the acetous fermentation has begun, the loaf,
#Text=when baked, will be "sad" and heavy, since there is no longer any gas to
#Text=puff it up.
#Text=If, however, during the first or alcoholic stage of
#Text=fermentation, new material be added, the same kind of fermentation will
#Text=continue for a certain period longer.
#Text=These facts serve to show that great care and attention are necessary to
#Text=produce good bread by a fermentative process.
#Text=If the fermentation has
#Text=not been allowed to proceed far enough to generate a sufficient amount
#Text=of gas to permeate the whole mass, the result will be a heavy loaf; and
#Text=if allowed to proceed too far, acid fermentation begins, the gas
#Text=escapes, and we have sour as well as heavy bread.
#Text=It is not enough,
#Text=however, to prevent bread from reaching the acetous or sour stage of
#Text=fermentation.
#Text=Bread may be over-fermented when there is no appreciable
#Text=sourness developed.
#Text=Fermentation may be carried so far as to destroy
#Text=much of the richness and sweetness of the loaf, and yet be arrested by
#Text=the baking process just before the acetous stage begins, so that it will
#Text=be light and porous, but decidedly lacking in flavor and substance.
#Text=Over-fermentation also develops in the bread various bitter substances
#Text=which obscure the natural sweetness of the bread and give to it an
#Text=unpleasant flavor.
#Text=Many of these substances are more or less harmful in
#Text=character, and include many poisons known as ptomaines, a class of
#Text=chemical compounds produced by germs whenever fermentation or
#Text=decomposition of organic matter takes place.
#Text=Much skill is required to
#Text=determine at what point to arrest the fermentation, in order to save the
#Text=sweetness and richness of the bread.
#Text=FERMENTATIVE AGENTS.
#Text=--Fermentation in vegetable matter is always
#Text=accompanied by the growth of living organisms.
#Text=The development of these
#Text=minute organisms is the exciting cause of fermentation and putrefaction.
#Text=The germs or spores of some of these fermenting agents are always
#Text=present in the air.
#Text=It is well known to housekeepers that if a batter of
#Text=flour and water and a little salt be kept in a jar of water at a
#Text=temperature of from 100° to 110°, it will ferment in the course of five
#Text=or six hours.
#Text=Scientists assure us that this fermentation is occasioned
#Text=by the introduction of the spores of certain species of fungi which are
#Text=continually floating in the atmosphere, and the proper conditions of
#Text=warmth and moisture being supplied, they at once begin to grow and
#Text=multiply.
#Text=This method of securing fermentation is utilized by housewives
#Text=in making what is termed salt-rising bread.
#Text=The raising of dough by this
#Text=process is lengthy and uncertain, and a far more convenient method is to
#Text=accelerate the fermentation by the addition of some active ferment.
#Text=The
#Text=ancient method of accomplishing this was by adding to the dough a
#Text=leaven, a portion of old dough which had been kept until it had begun to
#Text=ferment; but since the investigations of modern chemistry have made
#Text=clear the properties of yeast, that has come to be considered the best
#Text=agent for setting up the process of alcoholic fermentation in bread.
#Text=The
#Text=use of leaven is still practiced to somewhat in some European countries.
#Text=The bread produced with leaven, although light and spongy in texture,
#Text=has an unpleasant, sour taste, and is much less wholesome than that
#Text=produced with fresh yeast.
#Text=Yeast is a collection of living organisms or plants belonging to the
#Text=family of fungi, which, like all other plants, require warmth, moisture,
#Text=and food, in order to promote growth, and when properly supplied with
#Text=these, they begin to grow and multiply rapidly.
#Text=Fermentation will not
#Text=take place at a temperature below 30°, it proceeds slowly at 45°, but
#Text=from 70° to 90° it goes on rapidly.
#Text=Fermentation may be arrested by the
#Text=exhaustion of either the fermenting agent or the food supply, or by
#Text=exposure to heat at the temperature of boiling water.
#Text=This latter fact
#Text=enables the housewife to arrest the process of fermentation, when the
#Text=loaf has become sufficiently light, by baking it in a hot oven.
#Text=Heat
#Text=destroys most of the yeast cells; a few, however, remain in the loaf
#Text=unchanged, and it is for this reason that yeast bread is considered less
#Text=wholesome for dyspeptics than light unleavened bread.
#Text=It is apparent,
#Text=then, that the more thoroughly fermented bread is baked, the more
#Text=wholesome it will be, from the more complete destruction of the yeast
#Text=germs which it contains.
#Text=YEAST.
#Text=--Next to good flour, the most important requisite in the
#Text=manufacture of fermented bread is good yeast.
#Text=The best of flour used in
#Text=conjunction with poor yeast will not produce good bread.
#Text=The most
#Text=convenient and reliable kind of marketable yeast, when fresh, is the
#Text=compressed yeast.
#Text=The dry though they are always ready for use, the
#Text=quality of the bread they produce is generally inferior to that made
#Text=with either compressed yeast or good liquid yeast.
#Text=If this sort of yeast
#Text=must be depended upon, the cakes known as "Yeast Foam" are the best of
#Text=any with which we are acquainted.
#Text=Of homemade yeasts there are almost as many varieties as there are
#Text=cooks.
#Text=Their comparative value depends mainly upon the length of time
#Text=they will keep good, or the facility with which they can be prepared.
#Text=Essentially the same principles are involved in the making of them all;
#Text=viz., the introduction of a small quantity of fresh, lively yeast into a
#Text=mixture of some form of starch (obtained from flour, potato, or a
#Text=combination of both) and water, with or without the addition of such
#Text=other substances as will promote fermentation, or aid in preventing the
#Text=yeast from souring.
#Text=Under proper conditions of warmth, the small amount
#Text=of original yeast begins to supply itself with food at once by
#Text=converting the starch into dextrine, and then into grape sugar, and
#Text=multiplies itself with great rapidity, and will continue to do so as
#Text=long as there is material to supply it with the means of growth.
#Text=While
#Text=its growth is rapid, its decay is equally so; and unless some means of
#Text=preservation be employed, the yeast will die, and the mixture become
#Text=sour and foul.
#Text=Ordinarily it can be kept good for several days, and
#Text=under the best conditions, even three or four weeks.
#Text=After it has been
#Text=kept from four to six hours, it should be placed in some receptacle as
#Text=nearly air-tight as possible and set in the cellar or refrigerator,
#Text=where it can be kept at a temperature not conducive to fermentation.
#Text=Thus the little yeast organisms will remain in a quiescent state, but
#Text=yet alive and capable of multiplying themselves when again surrounded
#Text=with favorable conditions.
#Text=The yeast should be kept in glass or glazed earthen ware.
#Text=The vessel
#Text=containing it should be washed and scalded with scrupulous care before
#Text=new yeast is put in, since the smallest particle of sour or spoiled
#Text=yeast will ruin the fresh supply in a very short time.
#Text=It is generally
#Text=conceded that yeast will keep longer if the material of which it is made
#Text=be mixed with liquid of a boiling temperature, or cooked for a few
#Text=minutes at boiling heat before adding the yeast.
#Text=The reason for this
#Text=undoubtedly lies in the fact that the boiling kills foreign germs, and
#Text=thus prevents early souring or putrefaction.
#Text=The yeast must not be
#Text=added, however, until the liquid has cooled to a little more than blood
#Text=heat, as too great heat will kill the yeast cells.
#Text=The starch of the potato is thought to furnish better material for the
#Text=promotion of yeast growth than that of wheat flour; but whether the
#Text=potato be first cooked, mashed, and then combined with the other
#Text=ingredients, or grated raw and then cooked in boiling water, makes
#Text=little difference so far as results are concerned, though the latter
#Text=method may have the advantage of taking less time.
#Text=If potatoes are used
#Text=for this purpose, they should be perfectly mature.
#Text=New ones will not
#Text=answer.
#Text=Sugar assists in promoting the growth of the yeast plant, and a small
#Text=amount is usually employed in making yeast.
#Text=Hops serve to prevent the
#Text=yeast from souring, and an infusion of them is frequently used for this
#Text=purpose.
#Text=While it is essential that the water used should be boiling, it is also
#Text=necessary that the mixture should cooled to a lukewarm temperature
#Text=before the introduction of the original yeast, as intense heat will kill
#Text=the yeast plant.
#Text=Freezing cold will likewise produced the same result.
#Text=While a cool temperature is one of the requisites for keeping yeast
#Text=fresh, care must be taken, especially in winter, that it does not get
#Text=chilled.
#Text=When yeast is needed for bread, it is always the best plan to take a cup
#Text=to the cellar or refrigerator for the desired quantity, and re-cover the
#Text=jar as quickly as possible.
#Text=A half hour in a hot kitchen would be quite
#Text=likely to spoiled it.
#Text=Always shake or stir the whole well before
#Text=measuring out the yeast.
#Text=In making yeast, used earthen bowls for mixing,
#Text=porcelain-lined or granite-ware utensils for boiling, and silver or
#Text=wooden spoons for stirring.
#Text=BITTER YEAST.
#Text=--It sometimes happens that an excessive use of hops
#Text=in the making of yeast gives to it so bitter a flavor as to communicate
#Text=a disagreeable taste to the bread.
#Text=To correct this bitterness, mix with
#Text=the yeast a considerable quantity of water, and let it stand for some
#Text=hours, when the thickest portion will have settled at the bottom.
#Text=The
#Text=water, which will have extracted much of the bitterness, can then be
#Text=turned off and thrown away.
#Text=Yeast also sometimes becomes a bitter from
#Text=long keeping.
#Text=Freshly burnt charcoal thrown into the yeast is said to
#Text=absorb the odors and offensive matter and render the yeast more sweet;
#Text=however, we do not recommend the use of any yeast so stale as to need
#Text=sweetening or purifying.
#Text=Yeast that is new and fresh is always best; old
#Text=and stale yeast, even though it may still possess the property of
#Text=raising the dough, will give an unpleasant taste to the bread, and is
#Text=much less wholesome.
#Text=T
#Text=_DIRECTIONS FOR SERVING FRUITS._
#Text=
#Text=APPLES.
#Text=--In serving these, the "queen of all fruits," much
#Text=opportunity is afforded for a display of taste in their arrangement.
#Text=After wiping clean with a damp towel, they may be piled in a fruit
#Text=basket, with a few sprigs of green leaves here and there between their
#Text=rosy cheeks.
#Text=The feathery tops of carrots and celery are pretty for this
#Text=purpose.
#Text=Oranges and apples so arranged, make a highly ornamental dish.
#Text=Raw mellow sweet apples make a delicious dish when pared, sliced, and
#Text=served with cream.
#Text=BANANAS.
#Text=--Cut the ends from the fruit and serve whole, piled in a
#Text=basket with oranges, grapes, or plums.
#Text=Another way is to peel, slice,
#Text=and serve with thin cream.
#Text=Bananas are also very nice sliced, sprinkled
#Text=lightly with sugar, and before it had quite dissolved, covered with
#Text=orange juice.
#Text=Sliced bananas, lightly sprinkled with sugar, alternating
#Text=in layers with sections of oranges, make a most delicious dessert.
#Text=CHERRIES.
#Text=--Serve on stems, piled in a basket or high dish, with
#Text=bits of green leaves and vines between.
#Text=Rows of different colored
#Text=cherries, arranged in pyramidal form, make also a handsome dish.
#Text=CURRANTS.
#Text=--Large whole clusters may be served on the stem, and when
#Text=it is possible to obtain both red and white varieties, they make a most
#Text=attractive dish.
#Text=Put them into cold water for a little time, cool
#Text=thoroughly, and drain well before using.
#Text=Currants, if picked from the
#Text=stems after being carefully washed and drained, may be served lightly
#Text=sprinkled with sugar.
#Text=Currants and raspberries served together, half and
#Text=half, or one third currants two thirds raspberries, are excellent.
#Text=Only
#Text=the ripest of currants should be used.
#Text=GOOSEBERRIES.
#Text=--When fresh and ripe, the gooseberry is one of the
#Text=most delicious of small fruits.
#Text=Serve with stems on.
#Text=Drop into cold
#Text=water for a few moments, drain, and pile in a glass dish for the table.
#Text=GRAPES.
#Text=--Grapes need always to be washed before serving.
#Text=Drop the
#Text=bunches into ice water, let them remain ten of fifteen minutes, then
#Text=drain and serve.
#Text=An attractive dish may be made by arranging bunches of
#Text=different colored grapes together on a plate edged with grape leaves.
#Text=MELONS.
#Text=--Watermelons should be served very cold.
#Text=After being well
#Text=washed on the outside, put on ice until needed.
#Text=Cut off a slice at the
#Text=ends, that each half may stand upright on a plate, and then cut around
#Text=in even slices.
#Text=Instead of cutting through the center into even halves,
#Text=the melon may be cut in points back and forth around the entire
#Text=circumference, so that when separated, each half will appear like a
#Text=crown.
#Text=Another way is to take out the central portion with a spoon, in
#Text=cone-shaped pieces, and arrange on a plate with a few bits of ice.
#Text=Other
#Text=melons may be served in halves, with the seeds removed.
#Text=The rough skin
#Text=of the cantaloupe should be thoroughly scrubbed with a vegetable brush,
#Text=then rinsed and wiped, after which bury the melon in broken ice till
#Text=serving time; divide into eighths or sixteenths, remove the seeds,
#Text=reconstruct the melon, and serve surrounded with ice, on a folded
#Text=napkin, or arranged on a bed of grape leaves.
#Text=Do not cool the melon by
#Text=placing ice upon the flesh, as the moisture injures the delicate flavor.
#Text=ORANGES.
#Text=--Serve whole or cut the skin into eighths, halfway down,
#Text=separating it from the fruit, and curling it inward, thus showing half
#Text=the orange white and the other half yellow; or cut the skin into
#Text=eighths, two-thirds down, and after loosening from the fruit, leave them
#Text=spread open like the petals of a lily.
#Text=Oranges sliced and mixed with
#Text=well ripened strawberries, in the proportion of three oranges to a quart
#Text=of berries, make--a palatable dessert.
#Text=PEACHES AND PEARS.
#Text=--Pick out the finest, and wipe the wool from the
#Text=peaches.
#Text=Edge a plate with uniform sized leaves of foliage plant of the
#Text=same tints as the fruit, and pile the fruit artistically upon it,
#Text=tucking sprays or tips of the plant between.
#Text=Bits of ice may also be
#Text=intermingled.
#Text=Yellow Bartlett pears and rosy-cheeked peaches arranged in
#Text=this way are most ornamental.
#Text=THE PRESERVATION OF FRUIT.
#Text=Fresh fruit is so desirable, while at the same time the season during
#Text=which most varieties can be obtained is so transient, that various
#Text=methods are resorted to for preserving it in as nearly a natural state
#Text=as possible.
#Text=The old-fashioned plans of pickling in salt, alcohol, or
#Text=vinegar, or preserving in equal quantities of sugar, are eminently
#Text=unhygienic.
#Text=Quite as much to be condemned is the more modern process of
#Text=keeping fruit by adding to it some preserving agent, like salicylic acid
#Text=or other chemicals.
#Text=Salicylic acid is an antiseptic, and like many other
#Text=substances, such as carbolic acid, creosote, etc., has the power of
#Text=preventing the decay of organic substances.
#Text=Salicylic acid holds the
#Text=preference over other drugs of this class, because it imparts no
#Text=unpleasant flavor to the fruit.
#Text=It is nevertheless a powerful and
#Text=irritating drug, and when taken, even in small doses, produces intense
#Text=burning in the stomach, and occasions serious disturbances of the heart
#Text=and other organs.
#Text=Its habitual use produces grave diseases.
#Text=What is sold as antifermentive is simply the well-known antiseptic,
#Text=salicylate of soda.
#Text=It should be self-evident to one at all acquainted
#Text=with the philosophy of animal existence, that an agent which will
#Text=prevent fermentation and decay must be sufficiently powerful in its
#Text=influence to prevent digestion also.
#Text=The fermentation and decay of fruits as well as that of all other
#Text=organic substances, is occasioned by the action of those minute living
#Text=organisms which scientists call germs, and which are everywhere present.
#Text=These germs are very much less active in a dry, cold atmosphere, and
#Text=fruit may be preserved for quite a long period by refrigeration, an
#Text=arrangement whereby the external air is excluded, and the surrounding
#Text=atmosphere kept at an equal temperature of about 40° F.
#Text=The most
#Text=efficient and wholesome method of preserving fruit, however, is
#Text=destruction of the germs and entire exclusion from the air.
#Text=The germs
#Text=are destroyed at a boiling temperature; hence, if fruit be heated to
#Text=boiling, and when in this condition sealed in air-tight receptacles, it
#Text=will keep for an unlimited period.
#Text=CANNING FRUIT.
#Text=Canning consists in sealing in air-tight cans or jars, fruit which has
#Text=been previously boiled.
#Text=It is a very simple process, but requires a
#Text=thorough understanding of the scientific principles involved, and
#Text=careful management, to make it successful.
#Text=The result of painstaking
#Text=effort is so satisfactory, however, it is well worth all the trouble,
#Text=and fruit canning need not be a difficult matter if attention is given
#Text=to the following details:--
#Text=
#Text=Select self-sealing glass cans of some good variety.
#Text=Tin cans give more
#Text=trouble filling and sealing, are liable to affect the flavor of the
#Text=fruit, and unless manufactured from the best of material, to impair its
#Text=wholesomeness.
#Text=Glass cans may be used more than once, and are thus much
#Text=more economical.
#Text=Those with glass covers, or porcelain-lined covers, are
#Text=best.
#Text=Test the cans to see if they are perfect, with good rubbers and
#Text=covers that fit closely, by partly filling them with cold water,
#Text=screwing on the tops, and placing bottom upward upon the table for some
#Text=time before using.
#Text=If none of the water leaks out, they may be
#Text=considered in good condition.
#Text=If the cans have been previously used,
#Text=examine them with special care to see that both cans and covers have
#Text=been carefully cleaned, then thoroughly sterilize them, and fit with new
#Text=rubbers when necessary.
#Text=Cans and covers should be sterilized by boiling in water for half an
#Text=hour, or by baking in an oven, at a temperature sufficient to scorch
#Text=paper, for two hours.
#Text=The cans should be placed in the water or oven
#Text=when cold, and the temperature allowed to rise gradually, to avoid
#Text=breaking.
#Text=They should be allowed to cool gradually, for the same
#Text=purpose.
#Text=Select only the best of fruit, such as is perfect in flavor and neither
#Text=green nor over-ripe.
#Text=Fruit which has been shipped from a distance, and
#Text=which is consequently not perfectly fresh, contains germs in active
#Text=growth, and if the least bit musty, it will be almost sure to spoil,
#Text=even though the greatest care may be taken in canning.
#Text=Poor fruit will not be improved by canning; over-ripe fruit will be
#Text=insipid and mushy; and though cooking will soften hard fruit, it cannot
#Text=impart to it the delicate flavors which belong to that which is in its
#Text=prime.
#Text=The larger varieties of fruit should not be quite soft enough for
#Text=eating.
#Text=Choose a dry day for gathering, and put up at once, handling as
#Text=little as possible.
#Text=Try to keep it clean enough to avoid washing.
#Text=If the
#Text=fruit is to be pared, use a silver knife for the purpose, as steel is
#Text=apt to discolor the fruit.
#Text=If the fruit is one needing to be divided or
#Text=stoned, it will be less likely to become broken if divided before
#Text=paring.
#Text=Cook the fruit slowly in a porcelain-lined or granite-ware kettle, using
#Text=as little water as possible.
#Text=It is better to cook only small quantities
#Text=at a time in one kettle.
#Text=Steaming in the cans is preferable to stewing,
#Text=where the fruit is at all soft.
#Text=To do this, carefully fill the cans with
#Text=fresh fruit, packing it quite closely, if the fruit is large, and set
#Text=the cans in a boiler partly filled with cold water, with something
#Text=underneath them to prevent breaking,--muffin rings, straw, or thick
#Text=cloth, or anything to keep them from resting on the bottom of the boiler
#Text=(a rack made by nailing together strips of lath is very convenient);
#Text=screw the covers on the cans so the water cannot boil into them, but not
#Text=so tightly as to prevent the escape of steam; heat the water to boiling,
#Text=and steam the fruit until tender.
#Text=Peaches, pears, crab apples, etc., to
#Text=be canned with a syrup, may be advantageously cooked by placing on a
#Text=napkin dropped into the boiling syrup.
#Text=Fruit for canning should be so thoroughly cooked that every portion of
#Text=it will have been subjected to a sufficient degree of heat to destroy
#Text=all germs within the fruit, but overcooking should be avoided.
#Text=The
#Text=length of time required for cooking fruits for canning, varies with the
#Text=kind and quality of fruit and the manner of cooking.
#Text=Fruit is more
#Text=frequently spoiled by being cooked an insufficient length of time, than
#Text=by overcooking.
#Text=Prolonged cooking at a boiling temperature is necessary
#Text=for the destruction of certain kinds of germs capable of inducing
#Text=fermentation.
#Text=Fifteen minutes may be considered as the shortest time for
#Text=which even the most delicate fruits should be subjected to the
#Text=temperature of boiling water, and thirty minutes will be required by
#Text=most fruits.
#Text=Fruits which are not perfectly fresh, or which have been
#Text=shipped some distance, should be cooked not less than thirty minutes.
#Text=The boiling should be very slow, however, as hard, rapid boiling will
#Text=break up the fruit, and much of its fine flavor will be lost in the
#Text=steam.
#Text=Cooking the sugar with the fruit at the time of canning, is not to be
#Text=recommended from an economical standpoint; but fruit thus prepared is
#Text=more likely to keep well than when cooked without sugar; not, however,
#Text=because of the preservative influence of the sugar, which is too small
#Text=in amount to prevent the action of germs, as in the case of preserves,
#Text=but because the addition of sugar to the water or fruit juice increases
#Text=its specific gravity, and thus raises the boiling point.
#Text=From
#Text=experiments made, I have found that the temperature of the fruit is
#Text=ordinarily raised about 5° by the addition of the amount of sugar needed
#Text=for sweetening sub-acid fruit.
#Text=By the aid of this additional degree of
#Text=heat, the germs are more certainly destroyed, and the sterilization of
#Text=the fruit will be accomplished in a shorter time.
#Text=Another advantage gained in cooking sugar with the fruit at the time of
#Text=canning, is that the fruit may be cooked for a longer time without
#Text=destroying its form, as the sugar abstracts the juice of the fruit, and
#Text=thus slightly hardens it and prevents its falling in pieces.
#Text=The temperature to which the fruit is subjected may also be increased by
#Text=the same method as that elsewhere described for sterilizing milk, the
#Text=covers of the cans being screwed down tightly before they are placed in
#Text=the sterilizer, or as soon as the boiling point is approached, so that
#Text=the steam issues freely from the can.
#Text=See page 396.
#Text=If this method is
#Text=employed, it must be remembered that the cans should not be removed from
#Text=the sterilizer until after they have become cold, or nearly so, by being
#Text=allowed to stand over night.
#Text=Use the best sugar, two tablespoonfuls to a quart of fruit is
#Text=sufficient for most sub-acid fruits, as berries and peaches; plums,
#Text=cherries, strawberries, and currants require from five to eight
#Text=tablespoonfuls of sugar to a quart.
#Text=Have the sugar hot, by spreading it
#Text=on tins and heating in the oven, stirring occasionally.
#Text=See that; it
#Text=does not scorch.
#Text=Add it when the fruit is boiling.
#Text=Pears, peaches,
#Text=apples, etc., which contain a much smaller quantity of juice than do
#Text=berries, may be canned in a syrup prepared by dissolving a cup of sugar
#Text=in two or three cups of water.
#Text=Perfect fruit, properly canned, will keep
#Text=without sugar, and the natural 'flavor of the fruit is more perfectly
#Text=retained when the sugar is left out, adding the necessary amount when
#Text=opened for use.
#Text=If the fruit is to be cooked previous to being put in the cans, the cans
#Text=should be heated before the introduction of the fruit, which should be
#Text=put in at a boiling temperature.
#Text=Various methods are employed for this
#Text=purpose.
#Text=Some wrap the can in a towel wrung out of hot water, keeping a
#Text=silver spoon inside while it is being filled; others employ dry heat by
#Text=keeping the cans in a moderately hot oven while the fruit is cooking.
#Text=Another and surer way is to fill a large dishpan nearly full of scalding
#Text=(not boiling) water, then gradually introduce each can, previously
#Text=baked, into the water, dip it full of water, and set it right side up in
#Text=the pan.
#Text=Repeat the process with other cans until four or five are
#Text=ready.
#Text=Put the covers likewise into boiling water.
#Text=Have in readiness for
#Text=use a granite-ware funnel and dipper, also in boiling water; a cloth for
#Text=wiping the outside of the cans, a silver fork or spoon, a dish for
#Text=emptyings, and a broad shallow pan on one side of the range, half filled
#Text=with boiling water, in which to set the cans while being filled.
#Text=When
#Text=everything is in readiness, the fruit properly cooked, and _at a boiling
#Text=temperature,_ turn one of the cans down in the water, roll it over once
#Text=or twice, empty it, and set in the shallow pan of hot water; adjust the
#Text=funnel, and then place first in the can a quantity of juice, so that
#Text=when the fruit is put in, no vacant places will be left for air, which
#Text=is sometimes quite troublesome if this precaution is not taken; then
#Text=add the fruit.
#Text=If any bubbles of air chance to be left, work them out
#Text=with a fork or spoon handle, which first dip in boiling water, and then
#Text=quickly introduce down the sides of the jar and through the fruit in
#Text=such a way that not a bubble will remain.
#Text=Fill the can to overflowing,
#Text=remembering that any vacuum invites the air to enter; use boiling water
#Text=or syrup when there is not enough juice.
#Text=Skim all froth from the fruit,
#Text=adding more juice if necessary; wipe the juice from the top of the can,
#Text=adjust the rubber, put on the top, and screw it down as quickly as
#Text=possible.
#Text=If the fruit is cooked in the cans, as soon as it is
#Text=sufficiently heated, fill the can completely full with boiling juice,
#Text=syrup, or water; run the handle of a silver spoon around the inside of
#Text=the can, to make sure the juice entirely surrounds every portion of
#Text=fruit, and that no spaces for air remain, put on the rubbers, wipe off
#Text=all juice, and seal quickly.
#Text=As the fruit cools, the cover can be tightened, and this should be
#Text=promptly done again and again as the glass contracts, so that no air may
#Text=be allowed to enter.
#Text=If convenient to fill the cans directly from the stove, the fruit may be
#Text=kept at boiling heat by placing the kettle on a lamp stove on the table,
#Text=on which the other utensils are in readiness.
#Text=Many failures in fruit
#Text=canning are due to neglect to have the fruit boiling hot when put into
#Text=the cans.
#Text=When the cans are filled, set them away from currents of air, and not on
#Text=a very cold surface, to avoid danger of cracking.
#Text=A good way is to set
#Text=the cans on a wet towel, and cover with a woolen cloth as a protection
#Text=from draughts.
#Text=After the cans have cooled, and the tops have been screwed down tightly,
#Text=place them in a cool place, bottom upward, and watch closely for a few
#Text=days.
#Text=If the juice begins to leak out, or any appearance of fermentation
#Text=is seen, it is a sign that the work has failed, and the only thing to do
#Text=is to open the can immediately, boil the fruit, and use as quickly as
#Text=possible; recanning will not save it unless boiled a long time.
#Text=If no
#Text=signs of spoiling are observed within two or three weeks, the fruit may
#Text=be safely stored away in a dark, cool place.
#Text=If one has no dark
#Text=storeroom, it is an advantage to wrap each can in brown paper, to keep
#Text=out the light.
#Text=Sometimes the fruit will settle so that a little space appears at the
#Text=top.
#Text=If you are perfectly sure that the can is tight, do not open to
#Text=refill, as you will be unable to make it quite as tight again, unless
#Text=you reheat the fruit, in which case you would be liable to have the same
#Text=thing occur again.
#Text=Air is dangerous because it is likely to contain
#Text=germs, though in itself harmless.
#Text=If mold is observed upon the top of a can, it should be opened, and the
#Text=fruit boiled and used at once, after carefully skimming out all the
#Text=moldy portions.
#Text=If there is evidence of fermentation, the fruit should
#Text=be thrown away, as it contains alcohol.
#Text=If care be taken to provide good cans, thoroughly sterilized, and with
#Text=perfectly fitting covers; to use only fruit in good condition; to have
#Text=it thoroughly cooked, and at boiling temperature when put into the can;
#Text=to have the cans well baked and heated, filled completely and to
#Text=overflowing, and sealed at once while the fruit is still near boiling
#Text=temperature, there will be little likelihood of failure.
#Text=OPENING CANNED FRUIT.
#Text=--Canned fruit is best opened a short time
#Text=before needed, that is may be will aërated; and if it has been canned
#Text=without sugar, it should have the necessary quantity added, so that it
#Text=may be well dissolved before using.
#Text=Fruit purchased in tin cans should be selected with the utmost care,
#Text=since unscrupulous dealers sometimes use cans which render the fruit
#Text=wholly--unfit for food.
#Text=The following rules which we quote from a popular scientific journal
#Text=should be 'carefully observed in selecting canned fruit:--
#Text=
#Text="Reject every can that does not have the name of the manufacturer or
#Text=firm upon it, as well as the name of the company and the town where
#Text=manufactured.
#Text=resin around the edge of the solder of the cap, the same as is seen on
#Text=the seam at the side of the can.
#Text=will rattle the same as the bottom of your sewing-machine oil can does.
#Text=head of the can.
#Text=
#Text=
#Text=_RECIPES._
#Text=
#Text=TO CAN STRAWBERRIES.
#Text=specks; then cut the sections in which the "eyes" were, in solid pieces
#Text=clear down to the core.
#Text=By doing this all the valuable part of the fruit
#Text=is saved, leaving its hard, woody center.
#Text=As, however, this contains
#Text=considerable juice, it should be taken in the hands and wrung as one
#Text=wrings a cloth, till the juice is extracted, then thrown away.
#Text=Prepare a
#Text=syrup with one part sugar and two parts water, using what juice has been
#Text=obtained in place of so much water.
#Text=Let it boil up, skim clean, then add
#Text=the fruit.
#Text=Boil just as little as possible and have the fruit tender, as
#Text=pineapples loses its flavor by overcooking more readily than any other
#Text=fruit.
#Text=Put into hot cans, and seal.
#Text=LEGUMES
#Text=
#Text=The legumes, to which belong peas, beans, and lentils, are usually
#Text=classed among vegetables; but in composition they differ greatly from
#Text=all other vegetable foods, being characterized by a very large
#Text=percentage of the nitrogenous elements, by virtue of which they possess
#Text=the highest nutritive value.
#Text=Indeed, when mature, they contain a larger
#Text=proportion of nitrogenous matter than any other food, either animal or
#Text=vegetable.
#Text=In their immature state, they more nearly resemble the
#Text=vegetables.
#Text=On account of the excess of nitrogenous elements in their
#Text=composition, the mature legumes are well adapted to serve as a
#Text=substitute for animal foods, and for use in association with articles in
#Text=which starch or other non-nitrogenous elements are predominant; as, for
#Text=example, beans or lentils with rice, which combinations constitute the
#Text=staple food of large populations in India.
#Text=The nitrogenous matter of legumes is termed _legumin_, or vegetable
#Text=casein, and its resemblance to the animal casein of milk is very marked.
#Text=The Chinese make use of this fact, and manufacture cheese from peas and
#Text=beans.
#Text=The legumes were largely used as food by the ancient nations of
#Text=the East.
#Text=They were the "pulse" upon which the Hebrew children grew so
#Text=fair and strong.
#Text=According to Josephus, legumes also formed the chief
#Text=diet of the builders of the pyramids.
#Text=They are particularly valuable as
#Text=strength producers, and frequently form a considerable portion of the
#Text=diet of persons in training as athletes, at the present day.
#Text=Being foods
#Text=possessed of such high nutritive value, the legumes are deserving of a
#Text=more extended use than is generally accorded them in this country.
#Text=In
#Text=their mature state they are, with the exception of beans, seldom found
#Text=upon the ordinary bill of fare, and beans are too generally served in a
#Text=form quite difficult of digestion, being combined with large quantities
#Text=of fat, or otherwise improperly prepared.
#Text=Peas and lentils are in some
#Text=respects superior to beans, being less liable to disagree with persons
#Text=of weak digestion, and for this reason better suited to form a staple
#Text=article of diet.
#Text=All the legumes are covered with a tough skin, which is in itself
#Text=indigestible, and which if not broken by the cooking process or by
#Text=thorough mastication afterward, renders the entire seed liable to pass
#Text=through the digestive tract undigested, since the digestive fluids
#Text=cannot act upon the hard skin.
#Text=Even when the skins are broken, if served
#Text=with the pulp, much of the nutritive material of the legume is wasted,
#Text=because it is impossible for the digestive processes to free it from the
#Text=cellulose material of which the skins are composed.
#Text=If, then, it be
#Text=desirable to obtain from the legumes the largest amount of nutriment and
#Text=in the most digestible form, they must be prepared in some manner so as
#Text=to reject the skins.
#Text=Persons unable to use the legumes when cooked in
#Text=the ordinary way, usually experience no difficulty whatever in digesting
#Text=them when divested of their skins.
#Text=The hindrance which even the
#Text=partially broken skins are to the complete digestion of the legume, is
#Text=well illustrated by the personal experiments of Prof.
#Text=Strümpell, a
#Text=German scientist, who found that of beans boiled with the skins on he
#Text=was able to digest only 60 per cent of the nitrogenous material they
#Text=contained.
#Text=When, however, he reduced the same quantity of beans to a
#Text=fine powder previous to cooking, he was enabled to digest 91.8 per cent
#Text=of it.
#Text=The fact that the mature legumes are more digestible when prepared in
#Text=some manner in which the skins are rejected, was doubtless understood in
#Text=early times, for we find in a recipe of the fourteenth century,
#Text=directions given "to dry legumes in an oven and remove the skins away
#Text=before using them."
#Text=The green legumes which are more like a succulent vegetable are easily
#Text=digested with the skins on, if the hulls are broken before being
#Text=swallowed.
#Text=There are also some kinds of beans which, in their mature
#Text=state, from having thinner skins, are more readily digested, as the
#Text=Haricot variety.
#Text=SUGGESTIONS FOR COOKING.
#Text=--The legumes are best cooked by stewing or
#Text=boiling, and when mature, require prolonged cooking to render them
#Text=tender and digestible.
#Text=Slow cooking, when practicable, is preferable.
#Text=Dry beans and peas are more readily softened by cooking if first soaked
#Text=for a time in cold water.
#Text=The soaking also has a tendency to loosen the
#Text=skins, so that when boiled or stewed, a considerable portion of them
#Text=slip off whole, and being lighter, rise to the top during the cooking,
#Text=and can be removed with a spoon; it likewise aids in removing the strong
#Text=flavor characteristic of these foods, which is considered objectionable
#Text=by some persons.
#Text=The length of time required for soaking will depend
#Text=upon the age of the seed, those from the last harvest needing only a few
#Text=hours, while such as have been kept for two or more years require to be
#Text=soaked twelve or twenty-four hours.
#Text=For cooking, soft water is best.
#Text=The
#Text=mineral elements in hard water have a tendency to harden the casein, of
#Text=which the legumes a largely composed, thus rendering it often very
#Text=difficult to soften them.
#Text=The dry, unsoaked legumes are generally best put to cook in cold water,
#Text=and after the boiling point is reached, allowed to simmer gently until
#Text=done.
#Text=Boiling water may be used for legumes which have been previously
#Text=soaked.
#Text=The amount of water required will vary somewhat with the heat
#Text=employed and the age and condition of the legume, as will also the time
#Text=required for cooking, but as a general rule two quarts of soft water
#Text=for one pint of seeds will be quite sufficient.
#Text=Salt should not be added
#Text=until the seeds are nearly done, as it hinders the cooking process.
#Text=APPLE DESSERT.
#Text=--Pare some large tart apples, remove the cores, put
#Text=into the cavities a little quince jelly, lemon flavored sugar, or grated
#Text=pineapple and sugar, according to the flavor desired.
#Text=Have as many
#Text=squares of bread with the crust taken off as there are apples, and place
#Text=a filled apple on each piece of bread, on earthen pie plates; moisten
#Text=well with a little quince jelly dissolved in water, lemon juice, or
#Text=pineapple juice, according to the filling used.
#Text=Cover closely, and bake
#Text=in a rather quick oven till the apples are tender.
#Text=Serve with whipped
#Text=cream and sugar.
#Text=APPLE MERINGUE DESSERT.
#Text=--Pare and core enough tart, easy-cooking
#Text=apples to make a quart when stewed.
#Text=Cover closely and cook slowly till
#Text=perfectly tender, when they should be quite dry.
#Text=Mash through a
#Text=colander, add a little sugar and a little grated pineapple or lemon
#Text=peel.
#Text=Beat light with a silver fork, turn into a pudding dish, and brown
#Text=in a moderate oven ten or fifteen minutes.
#Text=Then cover with a meringue
#Text=made with two tablespoonfuls of sugar and the beaten whites of two eggs,
#Text=and return to the oven for a moment to brown.
#Text=Serve cold.
#Text=RICE PUDDING WITH RAISINS.
#Text=--Wash thoroughly one half cup of rice,
#Text=and soak for two hours in warm water.
#Text=Drain off the water, add two
#Text=tablespoonfuls of sugar, one half cup of raisins, and four cups of milk.
#Text=Put in an earthen pudding dish and cook for two hours in a moderate
#Text=oven, stirring once or twice before the rice begins to swell, then add a
#Text=cup of hot milk, and cook for an hour longer.
#Text=MEATS
#Text=
#Text=This is the term usually applied to the flesh and various organs of such
#Text=animals, poultry, and game as are used for food.
#Text=This class of foods
#Text=contains representatives of all nutritive elements, but is especially
#Text=characterized by as excess of albuminous matter.
#Text=But in actual nutritive
#Text=value flesh foods do not exceed various other food materials.
#Text=A
#Text=comparison of the food grains with beefsteak and other flesh foods,
#Text=shows, in fact, that a pound of grain is equivalent in food value to two
#Text=or three pounds of flesh.
#Text=At present time there is much question in the minds of many intelligent,
#Text=thinking people as to the propriety of using foods of this class, and
#Text=especially of their frequent use.
#Text=Besides being in no way superior to
#Text=vegetable substances, they contain elements of an excrementitious
#Text=character, which cannot be utilized, and which serve only to clog and
#Text=impede the vital processes, rendering the blood gross, filling the body
#Text=with second-hand waste material which was working its way out of the
#Text=vital domain of the animal when slaughtered.
#Text=To this waste matter,
#Text=consisting of unexpelled excretions, are added those produced by the
#Text=putrefactive processes which so quickly begin in flesh foods exposed to
#Text=air and warmth.
#Text=That flesh foods are stimulating has been shown by many observations and
#Text=experiments.
#Text=Flesh foods are also specially liable to be diseased and to communicate
#Text=to the consumer the same disease.
#Text=The prevalence of disease among
#Text=animals used for food is known to be very great, and their transmission
#Text=to man is no longer a matter of dispute.
#Text=It has been abundantly proved
#Text=that such diseases as the parasitic, tuberculous, erysipelatous, and
#Text=foot and mouth diseases are most certainly communicable to man by
#Text=infected flesh.
#Text=All stall and sty fed animals are more or less diseased.
#Text=Shut up in the dark, cut off from exercise, the whole fattening process
#Text=is one of progressive disease.
#Text=No living creature could long retain good
#Text=health under such unnatural and unwholesome conditions.
#Text=Add to this the
#Text=exhaustion and abuse of animals before slaughtering; the suffering
#Text=incident to long journeys in close cars, often without sufficient food
#Text=and water; and long drives over dusty roads under a burning sun to the
#Text=slaughter house, and it will be apparent to all thoughtful persons that
#Text=such influences are extremely liable to produce conditions of the system
#Text=that render the flesh unfit for food.
#Text=Thousands of animals are consumed each year which were slaughtered just
#Text=in time to save them from dying a natural death.
#Text=It is a common thing
#Text=for cattle owners, as soon as an animal shows symptoms of decline, to
#Text=send it to the butcher at once; and when epidemics of cattle diseases
#Text=are prevalent, there can be no doubt that the meat markets are flooded
#Text=with diseased flesh.
#Text=There are few ways in which we can more effectually imperil our health
#Text=than in partaking freely of diseased animal food.
#Text=This is no new theory.
#Text=The Jews have for ages recognized this danger, and their laws require
#Text=the most careful examination of all animals to be used as food, both
#Text=before and after slaughtering.
#Text=Their sanitary regulations demand that
#Text=beast or fowl for food must be killed by bleeding through the jugular
#Text=vein, and not, according to custom, by striking on the head, or in some
#Text=violent way.
#Text=Prior to the killing, the animal must be well rested and
#Text=its respiration normal; after death the most careful dissection and
#Text=examination of the various parts are made by a competent person, and no
#Text=flesh is allowed to be used for food which has not been inspected and
#Text=found to be perfectly sound and healthy.
#Text=As a result, it is found in
#Text=many of our large cities that only about one in twenty of the animals
#Text=slaughtered is accepted as food for a Jew.
#Text=The rejected animals are sold
#Text=to the general public, who are less scrupulous about the character of
#Text=their food, and who are in consequence more subject to disease and
#Text=shorter-lived than are Jews.
#Text=Trichinæ, tapeworms, and various other parasites which infest the flesh
#Text=of animals, are so common that there is always more or less liability to
#Text=disease from these sources among consumers of flesh foods.
#Text=Meat is by no means necessary for the proper maintenance of life or
#Text=vigorous health, as is proved by the fact that at least "four tenths of
#Text=the human race," according to Virey, "subsist exclusively upon a
#Text=vegetable diet, and as many as seven tenths are practically
#Text=vegetarians."
#Text=Some of the finest specimens of physical development and
#Text=mental vigor are to be found among those who use very little or no
#Text=animal food.
#Text=Says St.
#Text=Pierre, a noted French author, "The people living
#Text=upon vegetable foods are of all men the handsomest, the most vigorous,
#Text=the lease exposed to disease and passion; and they are those whose lives
#Text=last longest."
#Text=The use of large quantities of animal food, however free from disease
#Text=germs, has a tendency to develop the animal propensities to a greater or
#Text=less degree, especially in the young, whose characters are unformed.
#Text=Among animals we find the carnivorous the most vicious and destructive,
#Text=while those which subsist upon vegetable foods are by nature gentle and
#Text=tractable.
#Text=There is little doubt that this law holds good among men as
#Text=well as animals.
#Text=If we study the character and lives of those who
#Text=subsist largely upon animal food, we are apt to find them impatient,
#Text=passionate, fiery in temper, and in other respects greatly under the
#Text=dominion of their lower natures.
#Text=There are many other objections to the use of this class of foods--so
#Text=many in fact that we believe the human race would be far healthier,
#Text=better, and happier if flesh foods were wholly discarded.
#Text=If, however,
#Text=they are to be used at all, let them be used sparingly and prepared in
#Text=the simplest and least harmful manner.
#Text=Let them be cooked and served in
#Text=their own juices, not soaked in butter or other oils, or disguised by
#Text=the free use of pepper, mustard, catsup, and other pungent sauces.
#Text=Salt
#Text=also should be used only in the smallest possible quantities, as it
#Text=hardens the fiber, rendering it more difficult of digestion.
#Text=We can conceive of no possible stretch of hygienic laws which admits the
#Text=use of pork; so we shall give it and its products no consideration in
#Text=our pages.
#Text=Such offal as calves' brains, sheep's kidneys, beef livers, and other
#Text=viscera, is not fit food for any one but a scavenger.
#Text=The liver and
#Text=kidneys are depurating organs, and their use as food is not only
#Text=unwholesome but often exceedingly poisonous.
#Text=Meat pies, scallops, sauces, fricassees, _pâtés_, and other fancy dishes
#Text=composed of a mixture of animal foods, rich pastry, fats, strong
#Text=condiments, etc., are by no means to be recommended as hygienic, and
#Text=will receive no notice in these pages.
#Text=In comparative nutritive value, beef ranks first among the flesh foods.
#Text=Mutton, though less nutritive, is more easily digested than beef.
#Text=This
#Text=is not appreciable to a healthy person, but one whose digestive powers
#Text=are weak will often find that mutton taxes the stomach less than beef.
#Text=Veal or lamb is neither so nutritious nor so easily digested as beef or
#Text=mutton.
#Text=Flesh from different animals, and that from various parts of the
#Text=same animal, varies in flavor, composition, and digestibility.
#Text=The mode
#Text=of life and the food of animals influence in a marked manner the quality
#Text=of the meat.
#Text=Turnips give a distinctly recognizable flavor to mutton.
#Text=The same is true of many fragrant herbs found by cattle feeding in
#Text=pastures.
#Text=THE SELECTION OF MEAT.
#Text=--Good beef is of a reddish-brown color and
#Text=contains no clots of blood.
#Text=A pale-pink color indicates that the animal
#Text=was diseased; a dark-purple color that the animal has suffered from some
#Text=acute febrile affection or was not slaughtered, but died with the blood
#Text=in its body.
#Text=Good beef is firm and elastic to the touch; when pressed with the
#Text=finger, no impression is left.
#Text=It should be so dry upon the surface as
#Text=scarcely to moisten the fingers.
#Text=Meat that is wet, sodden, and flabby
#Text=should not be eaten.
#Text=Good beef is marbled with spots of white fat.
#Text=The
#Text=suet should be dry and crumble easily.
#Text=If the fat has the appearance of
#Text=wet parchment or is jelly-like, the beef is not good.
#Text=Yellow fat is an
#Text=indication of old, lean animals.
#Text=Good beef has little or no odor.
#Text=If any odor is perceptible, it is not
#Text=disagreeable.
#Text=Diseased meat has a sickly odor, resembling the breath of
#Text=feverish persons.
#Text=When such meat is roasted, it emits a strong,
#Text=offensive smell.
#Text=The condition of a piece of beef may be ascertained by
#Text=dipping a knife in hot water, drying it, and passing it through the
#Text=meat.
#Text=Apply to the nose on withdrawal, and if the meat is not good, a
#Text=disagreeable odor will be quite perceptible.
#Text=Good beef will not shrink greatly in cooking.
#Text=In boiling or stewing, the
#Text=shrinkage is computed to be about one pound in four; in baking, one and
#Text=one fourth pounds in four.
#Text=Beef of a close, firm fiber shrinks less than
#Text=meat of coarse fiber.
#Text=Good veal is slightly reddish or pink, and the fat should be white and
#Text=clear.
#Text=Avoid veal without fat, as such is apt to be too young to be
#Text=wholesome.
#Text=Good mutton should be firm and compact, the flesh, fine-grained and
#Text=bright-red, with an accumulation of very hard and clear white fat along
#Text=the borders of the muscles.
#Text=Meat should not be kept until decomposition sets in, as by the
#Text=putrefaction of the albuminous elements certain organic poisons are
#Text=generated, and flesh partaken of in this condition is liable to result
#Text=in serious illness.
#Text=Meat containing white specks is probably infested by
#Text=parasites and should not be used as food.
#Text=PRESERVATION OF MEAT.
#Text=--The tendency of flesh foods to rapid
#Text=decomposition has led to the use of various antiseptic agents and other
#Text=methods for its preservation.
#Text=One of the most common methods is that of immersion in a brine made of a
#Text=solution of common salt to which a small portion of saltpeter has been
#Text=added.
#Text=This abstracts the juice from the meat and also lessens the
#Text=tendency to putrefaction.
#Text=Salt is used in various other ways for
#Text=preserving meat.
#Text=It should be remarked, however, that cured and dried
#Text=meats are much more difficult to digest than fresh meat, and the nature
#Text=of the meat itself is so changed by the process as to render its
#Text=nutritive value much less.
#Text=Meat is sometimes packed in salt and afterward dried, either in the sun
#Text=or in a current of dry air.
#Text=Both salting and smoking are sometimes
#Text=employed.
#Text=By these means the juices are abstracted by the salt, and at
#Text=the same time the flesh is contracted and hardened by the action of
#Text=creosote and pyroligneous acid from the smoke.
#Text=What is termed "jerked" beef is prepared by drying in a current of warm
#Text=air at about 140°.
#Text=This dried meat, when reduced to a powder and packed
#Text=in air-tight cans, may be preserved for a long time.
#Text=When mixed with
#Text=fat, it forms the pemmican used by explorers in Arctic voyages.
#Text=Meat is also preserved by cooking and inclosing in air-tight cans after
#Text=the manner of canning fruit.
#Text=This process is varied in a number of ways.
#Text=The application of cold has great influence in retarding decomposition,
#Text=and refrigeration and freezing are often employed for the preservation
#Text=of flesh foods.
#Text=All of these methods except the last are open to the objection that
#Text=while they preserve the meat, they greatly lessen its nutritive value.
#Text=It should also be understood that the decomposition of its flesh begins
#Text=almost the moment an animal dies, and continues at a slow rate even when
#Text=the flesh is kept at a low temperature.
#Text=The poisons resulting from this
#Text=decomposition are often deadly, and are always detrimental to health.
#Text=THE PREPARATION AND COOKING OF MEAT.
#Text=--Meat, when brought from the
#Text=market, should be at once removed from the paper in which it is wrapped,
#Text=as the paper will absorb the juices of the meat; and if the wrapping is
#Text=brown paper, the meat is liable to taste of it.
#Text=Joints of meat should
#Text=not be hung with the cut surface down, as the juices will be wasted.
#Text=Meat kept in a refrigerator should not be placed directly on the ice,
#Text=but always upon plates or shelves, as the ice will freeze it or else
#Text=draw out its juices.
#Text=If meat is accidentally frozen, it should be thoroughly thawed in cold
#Text=water before cutting.
#Text=Meat should not be cleaned by washing with water,
#Text=as that extracts the nutritive juices, but by thoroughly wiping the
#Text=outside with a damp cloth.
#Text=The inside needs no cleaning.
#Text=Meat may be cooked by any of the different methods of cookery,--boiling,
#Text=steaming, stewing, roasting, broiling, baking, etc.,--according as the
#Text=object is to retain the nutriment wholly within the meat; to draw it all
#Text=out into the water, as in soups or broths; or to have it partly in the
#Text=water and partly in the meat, as in stews.
#Text=Broiling is, however,
#Text=generally conceded to be the most wholesome method, but something will
#Text=necessarily depend upon the quality of the meat to be cooked.
#Text=Meat which has a tough, hard fiber will be made tenderest by slow,
#Text=continuous cooking, as stewing.
#Text=Such pieces as contain a large amount of
#Text=gelatine--a peculiar substance found in the joints and gristly parts of
#Text=meat, and which hardens in a dry heat--are better stewed than roasted.
#Text=BOILING.
#Text=--The same principles apply to the boiling of all kinds of
#Text=meats.
#Text=The purpose to be attained by this method is to keep the
#Text=nutritive juices so far as possible intact within the meat;
#Text=consequently, the piece to be cooked should be left whole, so that only
#Text=a small amount of surface will be exposed to the action of the water.
#Text=Since cold water extracts albumen, of which the juices of the meat are
#Text=largely composed, while hot water coagulates it, meat to be boiled
#Text=should be plunged into boiling water sufficient to cover it and kept
#Text=there for five or ten minutes, by which time the albumen over the entire
#Text=surface will have become hardened, thus forming a coat through which
#Text=the juices cannot escape.
#Text=Afterward the kettle, closely covered, may be
#Text=set aside where the water will retain a temperature of about 180°.
#Text=A
#Text=small portion of albumen from the outer surface will escape into the
#Text=water in the form of scum, and should be removed.
#Text=Meat cooked in this way will require a longer time than when the water
#Text=is kept boiling furiously, but it is superior in every respect and more
#Text=digestible.
#Text=Something depends upon the shape of the piece cooked, thin
#Text=pieces requiring less time than a thick, cubical cut; but approximately,
#Text=first allowing fifteen or twenty minutes for the heat to penetrate the
#Text=center of the meat, at which time the real process of cooking begins, it
#Text=will require from twelve to fifteen minutes for every pound cooked.
#Text=STEWING.
#Text=--While the object in boiling is to preserve the juices
#Text=within the meat as much as possible, in stewing, the process is largely
#Text=reversed; the juices are to be partly extracted.
#Text=Some of the juices
#Text=exist between the fibers, and some are found within the fibers.
#Text=The
#Text=greater the surface exposed, the more easily these juices will be
#Text=extracted; hence meat for stewing should be cut into small pieces and
#Text=cooked in a small quantity of water.
#Text=Since cold water extracts the
#Text=albuminous juices, while boiling water hardens them into a leathery
#Text=consistency, water used for stewing should be neither cold nor boiling,
#Text=but of a temperature which will barely coagulate the albumen and retain
#Text=it in the meat in as tender a condition as possible; _i.e.,_ about 134°
#Text=to 160°.
#Text=To supply this temperature for the prolonged process of cooking
#Text=necessary in stewing, a double boiler of some form is quite necessary.
#Text=Put the pieces of meat to be stewed in the inner dish, add hot water
#Text=enough to cover, fill the outer boiler with hot water, and let this
#Text=outer water simmer very gently until the meat is perfectly tender.
#Text=The
#Text=length of time required will be greater than when meat is stewed
#Text=directly in simmering water, but the result will be much more
#Text=satisfactory.
#Text=The juices should be served with the meat.
#Text=STEAMING.
#Text=--Meat is sometimes steamed over boiling water until it
#Text=is made very tender and afterward browned in the oven.
#Text=Another method of steaming, sometimes called smothering, is that of
#Text=cooking meat in a tightly covered jar in a moderate oven for an hour
#Text=(the moderate heat serves to draw out the juice of the meat), after
#Text=which the heat is increased, and the meat cooked in its own juices one
#Text=half hour for each pound.
#Text=ROASTING.
#Text=--This method, which consists in placing meat upon a
#Text=revolving spit and cooking it before an open fire, is much less employed
#Text=now than formerly, when fireplaces were in general use.
#Text=What is
#Text=ordinarily termed roasting is in reality cooking meat it in own juices
#Text=in a hot oven.
#Text=In cooking meat by this method it is always desirable to
#Text=retain the juices entirely within the meat, which can be best
#Text=accomplished by first placing the clean-cut sides of the meat upon a
#Text=smoking-hot pan over a quick fire; press the meat close to the pan until
#Text=well scared and slightly browned, then turn over and sear the opposite
#Text=side in the same manner.
#Text=This will form a coating of hardened albumen,
#Text=through which the interior juices cannot escape.
#Text=Put at once into the
#Text=oven, arrange the fire so that the heat will be firm and steady but not
#Text=too intense, and cook undisturbed until tender.
#Text=Basting is not necessary if the roast is carefully seared and the oven
#Text=kept at proper temperature.
#Text=When the heat of the oven is just right, the
#Text=meat will keep up a continuous gentle sputtering in the pan.
#Text=If no
#Text=sputtering can be heard, the heat is insufficient.
#Text=The heat is too great
#Text=when the drippings burn and smoke.
#Text=BROILING.
#Text=--This is the method employed for cooking thin cuts of
#Text=meat in their own juices over glowing coals.
#Text=When properly done, broiled
#Text=meat contains a larger amount of uncoagulated albumen than can be
#Text=secured by cooking in any other manner; hence it is the most wholesome.
#Text=For broiling, a bed of clear, glowing coals without flame is the first
#Text=essential.
#Text=Coke, charcoal, or anthracite coal serves best for securing
#Text=this requisite.
#Text=In an ordinary stove, the coals should be nearly to the top of the
#Text=fire-box, that the meat may be held so as almost to touch the fire.
#Text=No
#Text=utensil is better for ordinary purposes than a double wire broiler.
#Text=First, rub it well with a bit of suet, then put in the meat with the
#Text=thickest part in the center.
#Text=Wrap a coarse towel around the hand to
#Text=protect it from the heat, hold the meat as near the fire as possible, so
#Text=as to sear one side instantly, slowly count ten, then turn and sear the
#Text=other side.
#Text=Continue the process, alternating first one side and then
#Text=the other, slowly counting ten before each turning, until the meat is
#Text=sufficiently done.
#Text=Successful broiling is largely dependent upon
#Text=frequent turning.
#Text=The heat, while it at once sears the surface, starts
#Text=the flow of the juices, and although they cannot escape through the
#Text=hardened surface, if the meat were entirely cooked on one side before
#Text=turning, they would soon come to the top, and when it was turned over,
#Text=would drip into the fire.
#Text=If the meat is seared on both sides, the
#Text=juices will be retained within, unless the broiling is too prolonged,
#Text=when they will ooze out and evaporate, leaving the meat dry and
#Text=leathery.
#Text=Salt draws out the juices, and should not be added until the
#Text=meat is done.
#Text=As long as meat retains its juices, it will spring up
#Text=instantly when pressed with a knife; when the juices have begun to
#Text=evaporate, it will cease to do this.
#Text=Broiled meats should be served on
#Text=hot dishes.
#Text=BEEF.
#Text=ECONOMY AND ADAPTABILITY IN SELECTION.
#Text=--While the greatest care
#Text=should be exercised in the selection of beef as regards its soundness
#Text=and wholesomeness, it must likewise be selected with reference to
#Text=economy and adaptability for cooking purposes, pieces from different
#Text=portions of the animal being suitable for cooking only in certain ways.
#Text=Ox beef is said to be best.
#Text=That beef is most juicy and tender which has
#Text=fine streaks of fat intermingled with the lean.
#Text=Beef which is
#Text=coarse-grained and hard to cut is apt to be tough.
#Text=An economical piece
#Text=of beef to purchase is the back of the rump.
#Text=It is a long piece with
#Text=only a small portion of bone, and weighs about ten pounds.
#Text=The thickest
#Text=portion may be cut into steaks, the thin, end with bone may be utilized
#Text=for soups and stews, while the remainder will furnish a good roast.
#Text=Only
#Text=a small portion of choice tender lean meat is to be found in one animal,
#Text=and these are also the most expensive; but the tougher, cheaper parts,
#Text=if properly cooked, are nearly as nutritious.
